nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still freshwater streams, swamps, and billabongs in lowland tropical and subtropical areas; heavily vegetated, often tannin-stained water with leaf litter over sandy or silty substrate

decorPreferences: dense planting along sides and back (Java fern, Vallisneria, Cryptocoryne)
open central swimming area for courtship displays
fine-leaved plants or spawning mops for egg deposition
floating plants to diffuse light and reduce skittishness
driftwood and leaf litter for natural feel

diet.notes: Readily accepts a wide variety of foods. High-quality small flake and micro-pellets form a good base; supplement regularly with small frozen or live foods such as daphnia, baby brine shrimp, micro-worms, and cyclops to enhance coloration and encourage spawning. Their small mouths require appropriately sized particles — crushed flake or micro-pellets are recommended. Spirulina-enriched foods help maintain vibrant coloration.

goodWith: small tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
other small rainbowfish (threadfin rainbowfish, forktail rainbowfish)
dwarf shrimp (cherry shrimp — adults generally safe; fry may be taken)
avoidWith: predatory fish that could swallow them whole
very boisterous species that outcompete them at feeding

summary: Macculloch’s Rainbowfish is one of the smallest and most beginner-friendly rainbowfish, reaching only 5–7 cm. Males display striking red-edged scales and red fin accents. Native to northern Australia and southern New Guinea; unusually cool-tolerant for a tropical rainbowfish.
careNotes: Macculloch’s Rainbowfish (Melanotaenia maccullochi) is one of the smallest members of the family Melanotaeniidae and is widely considered the most beginner-friendly rainbowfish available in the hobby. Males reach approximately 6–7 cm and females 5–6 cm, making them suitable for tanks as small as 60 L (60 cm footprint), though larger tanks reward keepers with more natural group behaviour and more vivid displays. A group of at least six (ideally more, with a slight female majority) brings out the best coloration and courtship activity in males. Like all rainbowfish, M. maccullochi is most colourful under bright lighting against a dark substrate, and the fish typically intensifies in colour significantly after the first few weeks in a well-maintained aquarium. One of the species’ most notable traits is its temperature tolerance: it thrives between 20–26°C and can tolerate brief dips toward 18°C, making it suitable for unheated tanks in temperate regions during warmer months — a rare quality among rainbowfish. Maintain slightly soft to moderately hard, near-neutral water (pH 6.5–7.5, gH 5–15). Weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are important, as rainbowfish are sensitive to accumulated nitrates. The species is an active mid-water swimmer and benefits from open swimming space in the centre of the tank with dense planting along the sides and back.
breedingNotes: Macculloch’s Rainbowfish are egg-scatterers that spawn readily in a well-planted aquarium. Males display intensified coloration and chase females during morning courtship. Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops and are adhesive. Unlike many egg-scatterers, adults show limited interest in eating deposited eggs, though some predation occurs. Eggs hatch in 7–10 days at 24°C; fry are tiny and require infusoria, green water, or commercial fry foods for the first week before graduating to baby brine shrimp and micro-worms. A separate breeding tank with a spawning mop, gentle sponge filtration, and slightly raised temperature (24–26°C) significantly improves fry survival rates. Conditioning with live and frozen foods promotes spawning.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Melanotaenia maccullochi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males to 7 cm), temperature range (20–26°C), pH (6.5–7.5), hardness (5–15 dH), native range (northern Queensland and southern New Guinea), habitat description (slow-moving streams, swamps), diet, breeding behaviour, and compatibility guidance.

additional.name: FishBase — Melanotaenia maccullochi
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Melanotaeniidae, Atheriniformes), adult size (to 7 cm TL), temperature range (20–26°C), native range (Queensland, Australia; southern Papua New Guinea), diet (omnivore), and depth data.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rainbowfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General rainbowfish care guide; supplemented with trade-level guidance on feeding (small foods for small mouths), group size recommendations, plant stocking, and water change frequency for rainbowfish. Confirmed beginner-friendly rating and omnivore diet. No dedicated M. maccullochi page available.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Melanotaenia maccullochi species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (up to 7 cm), cool-water tolerance (can tolerate down to 18°C), shoaling group size recommendation (6+), tank size guidance (60 cm minimum), and breeding notes (spawning mops, egg adhesion, fry care). Noted species’ suitability for cooler subtropical setups.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Dwarf Rainbowfish Melanotaenia maccullochi
additional.notes: Additional species coverage confirming common name ‘Dwarf Rainbowfish’, northern Queensland and southern New Guinea range, cool-temperature tolerance, beginner-level care rating, and omnivore diet with emphasis on small particle size. Breeding notes on egg-scattering among fine-leaved plants consistent with other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; spawns readily in planted tanks or with spawning mops. Eggs hatch in 7–10 days. Fry require infusoria or fry foods initially, then baby brine shrimp. Separate breeding tank improves fry survival.
